---
title: "Methodology and Technical Reference"
subtitle: "A block-exogenous Bayesian VAR suite for the Australian economy — functional forms, priors, estimation, forecasting, evaluation and combination"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    embed-resources: true
    html-math-method: mathjax
    fig-align: center
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

This document is the rigorous companion to the narrative report
(`report.html`) and the performance scorecard (`model_scorecard.md`). It
states every functional form, prior, posterior, sampling step, forecasting
recursion, scoring rule and combination formula used in the suite, with the
config key and default value for each tunable quantity. Notation is
consistent throughout and matches the source (`R/priors.R`, `R/engines.R`,
`R/covid.R`, `R/forecast.R`, `R/evaluate.R`, `R/combine.R`,
`R/benchmarks.R`). Cross-references of the form **Dk** point to the dated
decision log in `README.md`.

# The reduced-form VAR

## Levels form

Let $y_t \in \mathbb{R}^{M}$ be the vector of modelled variables at quarter
$t$ (in the transformed units of §2). Every VAR member is a reduced-form
Gaussian vector autoregression of order $p$,

$$
y_t \;=\; c \;+\; \sum_{l=1}^{p} A_l\, y_{t-l} \;+\; \varepsilon_t,
\qquad \varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\Sigma),
\qquad t = p+1,\dots,T,
$$ {#eq-var}

with intercept $c\in\mathbb{R}^M$, coefficient matrices
$A_l\in\mathbb{R}^{M\times M}$, and a full $M\times M$ innovation covariance
$\Sigma$. Lag length $p$ is a per-member choice (`suite[].lags`, values
$2,4,5$).

## Regression (stacked) form

Collect the $T-p$ usable observations. With the regressor row
$x_t = (1,\,y_{t-1}^\top,\dots,y_{t-p}^\top)^\top \in \mathbb{R}^{K}$,
$K = 1+Mp$, stack

$$
\underbrace{Y}_{(T-p)\times M} \;=\; \underbrace{X}_{(T-p)\times K}\,
\underbrace{B}_{K\times M} \;+\; E,
\qquad
B = \begin{pmatrix} c^\top \\ A_1^\top \\ \vdots \\ A_p^\top \end{pmatrix},
\qquad
\operatorname{vec}(E)\sim\mathcal N\!\big(0,\ \Sigma\otimes I\big).
$$ {#eq-regform}

Row $k$ of $B$ with $k = 1 + (l-1)M + j$ (for $k>1$) holds the loadings of
**lag $l$ of variable $j$** across the $M$ equations; column $i$ is
equation $i$. This indexing (`coef_index()` in `R/priors.R`) is used
throughout. `build_XY()` constructs $(Y,X)$.

## Companion form and stability

Stacking $\tilde y_t = (y_t^\top,\dots,y_{t-p+1}^\top)^\top$ gives the
$Mp\times Mp$ companion matrix

$$
\mathbf{A} =
\begin{pmatrix}
A_1 & A_2 & \cdots & A_p \\
I_M & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\
& \ddots & & \vdots \\
& & I_M & 0
\end{pmatrix}.
$$

The system is covariance-stationary iff $\rho(\mathbf A)\equiv\max_i
|\lambda_i(\mathbf A)| < 1$. `max_eig_mod()` computes $\rho(\mathbf A)$; it
drives the stability diagnostic (§11), the explosive-draw rejection in the
steady-state sampler (§6.4), and the stationarity truncation of the AR
benchmark (§8).

## The small-open-economy partition and block exogeneity {#sec-blockexog}

Partition $y_t = (f_t^\top, d_t^\top)^\top$ into a **foreign block**
$f_t\in\mathbb R^{n_f}$ (world activity, commodity prices, the foreign
policy rate) and a **domestic block** $d_t\in\mathbb R^{n_d}$,
$M=n_f+n_d$. Columns are ordered foreign-first
(`build_transform_spec()` sorts on `block`), so each lag matrix has the
partition

$$
A_l =
\begin{pmatrix}
A_l^{ff} & A_l^{fd} \\[2pt]
A_l^{df} & A_l^{dd}
\end{pmatrix}.
$$

**Block exogeneity** is the SOE identifying restriction
(Cushman–Zha 1997; Zha 1999): the foreign block does not respond to the
domestic block, i.e.

$$
A_l^{fd} = 0 \quad \text{for all } l=1,\dots,p .
$$ {#eq-blockexog}

Economically, Australia is a price-taker on world activity and commodity
prices; without @eq-blockexog the model would infer, from 30 years of
comovement, spurious channels in which Australian variables move world
demand — corrupting the medium-horizon dynamics that the iterated forecast
(§7) propagates. RBA RDP 2013-06 shows imposing it materially raises the
measured role of foreign shocks. Three engines impose it three ways
(§§4–6); one member (`small_unres`, **D19**) deliberately omits it as a
control. The restriction constrains the **lag dynamics** only; the
steady-state means remain coupled through $\Sigma$ (§6.4, **D3**).

The posterior-mean violation
$\max_{l}\, \| A_l^{fd}\|_{\max}$ is logged per fit
(`block_exog_metric()`) and gated below $10^{-2}$ (§11); structural-zero
engines report exactly $0$.

# Data, transforms and target measures {#sec-data}

## Series and transforms

The `transform_spec` table (`config.yml → variables`, assembled by
`build_transform_spec()`) is the single source of truth. Each raw series
$r_{v,t}$ is mapped to model units by one of three transforms
(`apply_transform()`):

$$
y_{v,t} =
\begin{cases}
100\,\big(\log r_{v,t} - \log r_{v,t-1}\big) & \text{transform } \texttt{dlog}\ \text{(quarterly \% growth)}\\[4pt]
100\,\log r_{v,t} & \text{transform } \texttt{loglevel}\\[4pt]
r_{v,t} & \text{transform } \texttt{level}.
\end{cases}
$$

Growth-type series (GDP, CPI, wages, employment, consumption, both
foreign activity measures, commodity prices) use `dlog`; interest and
unemployment rates use `level`; the real TWI and terms of trade use
`loglevel`. The panel is balanced to the common finite sample
(`transform_data()`); interior gaps error rather than impute.

## Minnesota own-lag prior centre $\delta$

Each variable carries a prior persistence $\delta_v$ (`variables[].delta`)
that centres its own first-lag coefficient (§3):

$$
\delta_v =
\begin{cases}
0 & \text{mean-reverting growth rates (}\texttt{dlog}\text{)}\\
1 & \text{persistent levels (}\texttt{level},\ \texttt{loglevel}\text{)} .
\end{cases}
$$

This is the textbook Litterman (1986) assignment: a random-walk prior for
$I(1)$ level variables, a white-noise prior for stationary growth rates
(**D2**).

## Target measures scored {#sec-measures}

For the four policy targets (GDP growth, trimmed-mean inflation,
unemployment, cash rate) three transforms of the predictive path are
scored (`score_member()`). Writing $y_{v,t+h}$ for the model-unit outcome
$h$ quarters after origin $t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{quarterly (q):}\quad & y_{v,t+h}, \\
\textbf{year-ended (ye):}\quad & \textstyle\sum_{j=0}^{3} y_{v,t+h-j}
&&\text{(dlog targets only; the RBA headline concept)}, \\
\textbf{cumulative level (cum):}\quad & \textstyle\sum_{j=1}^{h} y_{v,t+j}
&&\text{(dlog targets, } h\ge 2\text{; }=100\,\Delta\log\text{level over the horizon).}
\end{aligned}
$$

For `dlog` variables the **cum** measure is the headline: it is where the
price level / real GDP actually lands $h$ quarters out, and it compounds any
persistent bias in the quarterly forecast. For `level` variables (rates)
the level *is* the quarterly measure, so **q** serves as the level view.
The year-ended measure splices realized history when $h<4$; when it is
reconstructed from draws the same 4-quarter convention is used
(`.ye_from_q()`), preserving within-path dependence.

# The Minnesota prior family {#sec-minn}

Two representations are used, distinguished by whether the prior variance can
differ across equations.

## Independent-prior Minnesota moments {#sec-minn-indep}

For the Gibbs, steady-state and SV engines, the prior on each coefficient is
independent Gaussian with mean $b_0$ and standard deviation $s_0$
(`minnesota_prior()`), both $K\times M$. The intercept:

$$
b_0[1,i] = 0, \qquad s_0[1,i] = \theta_c\,\sigma_i,\qquad \theta_c = 10 .
$$

For the coefficient in equation $i$ on lag $l$ of variable $j$
(row $k=1+(l-1)M+j$):

$$
b_0[k,i] =
\begin{cases}\delta_i & i=j,\ l=1\\ 0 & \text{otherwise}\end{cases}
\qquad
s_0[k,i] = \lambda \cdot
\underbrace{\big(\mathbb 1_{i=j} + \kappa\,\mathbb 1_{i\ne j}\big)}_{\text{own vs cross}}
\cdot \frac{\sigma_i}{\sigma_j}\cdot l^{-\alpha}.
$$ {#eq-minn}

The four Minnesota levers:

| Symbol | Role | Value | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\lambda$ | overall tightness (own-lag sd at lag 1) | member-specific; `auto`, $0.05$, $0.4$ | §5, `prior.lambda` |
| $\kappa$ | cross-variable shrinkage | $0.5$ | `cross` |
| $\alpha$ | lag decay exponent ($s_0\propto l^{-\alpha}$) | $1$ | `lag_decay` |
| $\theta_c$ | intercept looseness (multiple of $\sigma_i$) | $10$ | `intercept_scale` |

The scale factors $\sigma_i$ are residual standard deviations from a
univariate AR($p$) fit to each series (`ar_sigmas()`), so shrinkage is in
units of each equation's own noise; the ratio $\sigma_i/\sigma_j$ makes the
prior invariant to the arbitrary scaling of the regressors. Under the COVID
treatment (§7) the same GLS row weights are applied when computing
$\sigma_i$, closing the outlier-contamination channel of the scale
calibration (Hartwig 2024, **D17**).

**Block exogeneity in the prior.** For a foreign equation $i$ ($f_t$
component) and any domestic variable $j$, @eq-minn is overridden by

$$
b_0[k,i] = 0, \qquad s_0[k,i] = \underline{s} = 10^{-4}
\quad(\texttt{mcmc.block\_exog\_prior\_sd}),
$$ {#eq-blockexog-prior}

a near-dogmatic spike at zero: prior mean *and* variance are ~0, so the
posterior for those coefficients is pinned at $0$ to numerical precision.
This asymmetric (equation-specific) prior variance is representable only
because the prior is independent across equations — which is exactly why
block exogeneity requires the Gibbs/SV engines and cannot be done with the
conjugate Kronecker prior below (**D3**).

## Conjugate Normal–inverse-Wishart prior {#sec-conj-prior}

For the fast closed-form engines (`conj_br`, and the GLP marginal likelihood
of §5), the prior is the natural conjugate matric-variate NIW
(`conjugate_prior()`):

$$
B\mid\Sigma \sim \mathcal{MN}_{K\times M}\!\big(B_0,\ \Omega_0\otimes\Sigma\big),
\qquad
\Sigma \sim \mathcal{IW}(S_0,\nu_0),
$$ {#eq-conj-prior}

i.e. $\operatorname{vec}(B)\mid\Sigma \sim \mathcal
N\!\big(\operatorname{vec}(B_0),\ \Sigma\otimes\Omega_0\big)$ with a
**diagonal** among-row scale $\Omega_0$:

$$
B_0[1+j,\,i]=\delta_i\ (\text{own first lag}),\quad
\Omega_0[1,1]=\theta_c^2,\quad
\Omega_0[k,k]=\Big(\frac{\lambda}{\sigma_j\, l^{\alpha}}\Big)^{2}.
$$

The prior on $\Sigma$ is centred at the diagonal AR-residual covariance:
$\nu_0 = M+2$ and $S_0 = \operatorname{diag}(\sigma^2)\,(\nu_0-M-1)$, so
$\mathbb E[\Sigma]=\operatorname{diag}(\sigma_1^2,\dots,\sigma_M^2)$.

The Kronecker structure $\Omega_0\otimes\Sigma$ forces the *same* row
covariance $\Omega_0$ on every equation — cross-shrinkage is effectively
fixed and block exogeneity cannot be imposed. That is the modelling reason
the suite carries both prior families.

# Long-run (dummy-observation) priors {#sec-soc-dio}

At $h=8\text{–}12$ an iterated forecast converges to the model's
unconditional mean, which a plain Minnesota prior pins down poorly for
$I(1)$ levels. Two artificial-observation priors discipline this, appended
as extra rows to $(Y,X)$ before estimation (`soc_dio_dummies()`, applied to
the members with `prior.soc/dio: true`). Let $\bar y$ be the mean of the
first $p$ observations.

**Sum-of-coefficients (Doan–Litterman–Sims).** One dummy row per level
variable $j$ (those with $\delta_j=1$), with tightness $\mu$
(`prior.soc_mu`, default $1$):

$$
y^\ast_j = \frac{\bar y_j}{\mu}\,e_j,\qquad
x^\ast_j = \frac{\bar y_j}{\mu}\,\big(0,\underbrace{e_j,\dots,e_j}_{p\text{ lag blocks}}\big),
$$

which shrinks the sum of own lag coefficients toward one (a unit root / no
deterministic drift) as $\mu\to 0$.

**Dummy-initial-observation (Sims–Zha).** A single row asserting that the
system starts at $\bar y$ and stays there, with tightness
$\delta_{\text{dio}}$ (`prior.dio_delta`, default $1$):

$$
y^\ast = \frac{\bar y}{\delta_{\text{dio}}},\qquad
x^\ast = \frac{1}{\delta_{\text{dio}}}\big(1,\ \bar y^\top,\dots,\bar y^\top\big),
$$

a single co-persistence / common-trend prior favouring the presence of unit
roots without over-differencing. Both are applied only to level variables;
applying them to growth rates would bias the well-estimated short-run
dynamics (**D5**). For the block-recursive engine the dummies are split by
block and the domestic dummy rows carry the contemporaneous-foreign columns
(§4.2).

# Data-driven shrinkage: GLP marginal-likelihood selection {#sec-glp}

Rather than hand-set $\lambda$, the members with `prior.lambda: auto` inherit
an overall tightness chosen by maximising the closed-form conjugate
log marginal likelihood (Giannone–Lenza–Primiceri 2015, **D4**). For the
conjugate NIW model the marginal likelihood is analytic
(`log_marginal_likelihood()`). With posterior quantities

$$
P_1 = \Omega_0^{-1} + X^\top X,\quad
B_1 = P_1^{-1}\big(\Omega_0^{-1}B_0 + X^\top Y\big),\quad
S_1 = S_0 + Y^\top Y + B_0^\top\Omega_0^{-1}B_0 - B_1^\top P_1 B_1,\quad
\nu_1 = \nu_0 + T,
$$ {#eq-post-conj}

the log marginal likelihood is

$$
\log p(Y\mid\lambda) =
-\frac{MT}{2}\log\pi
+\log\Gamma_M\!\Big(\tfrac{\nu_1}{2}\Big)
-\log\Gamma_M\!\Big(\tfrac{\nu_0}{2}\Big)
+\frac{\nu_0}{2}\log|S_0|
-\frac{\nu_1}{2}\log|S_1|
+\frac{M}{2}\Big(\!-\log|P_1| - \log|\Omega_0|\Big),
$$ {#eq-logml}

where $\log\Gamma_M$ is the multivariate log-gamma (`lmvgamma()`) and
$\log|P_1|$ is read off its Cholesky factor for stability. `select_lambda()`
evaluates @eq-logml on the grid $\{0.05,0.1,0.15,0.2,0.3,0.4,0.6\}$
(`glp.lambda_grid`) and returns the maximiser. Crucially this is **re-run at
every forecast origin inside the recursive loop** on the data up to that
origin, so the selection carries no look-ahead. Two members keep fixed
$\lambda$ ($0.05$ tight, $0.4$ loose) for suite diversity.

# Estimation engines {#sec-engines}

Four engines share the interface `fit(y, member, spec, cfg) → posterior`
and a `simulate_paths()` method (§7). Each returns draws
$\{(B^{(d)},\Sigma^{(d)})\}_{d=1}^{N}$ (or the SV analogue) and MCMC
diagnostics.

## Conjugate NIW posterior and sampler {#sec-conj-post}

Under @eq-conj-prior the posterior is conjugate:

$$
\Sigma\mid Y \sim \mathcal{IW}(S_1,\nu_1),\qquad
B\mid\Sigma,Y \sim \mathcal{MN}\!\big(B_1,\ \Omega_1\otimes\Sigma\big),\quad
\Omega_1 = P_1^{-1},
$$

with $B_1,S_1,\nu_1$ from @eq-post-conj. `sample_conjugate()` draws
i.i.d. from this in closed form. Writing $P_1 = R^\top R$ (upper Cholesky
$R$), each draw is

$$
\Sigma^{(d)} \sim \mathcal{IW}(S_1,\nu_1),\qquad
B^{(d)} = B_1 + R^{-1} Z\, \operatorname{chol}(\Sigma^{(d)}),
\quad Z_{K\times M}\ \text{i.i.d. } \mathcal N(0,1),
$$

so that $\operatorname{Var}\!\big(\operatorname{vec}(B^{(d)})\big) =
\Sigma^{(d)}\otimes (R^{-1}R^{-\top}) = \Sigma^{(d)}\otimes\Omega_1$ as
required.

**Inverse-Wishart draws** (`riwish()`) use the Bartlett decomposition: to
draw $\Sigma\sim\mathcal{IW}(S,\nu)$, factor $S^{-1}=LL^\top$, form the lower
triangular $\mathbf T$ with $\mathbf T_{ii}=\sqrt{\chi^2_{\nu-i+1}}$ and
$\mathbf T_{ij}\sim\mathcal N(0,1)$ for $i>j$, set
$W = L\,\mathbf T\mathbf T^\top L^\top \sim \mathcal W(S^{-1},\nu)$, and
return $W^{-1}$.

## Block-recursive conjugate engine `conj_br` {#sec-conjbr}

Block exogeneity is imposed here **structurally** by the recursive
factorisation $p(f_t,d_t) = p(f_t)\,p(d_t\mid f_t)$ (the RBNZ
Bloor–Matheson approach), so the fast conjugate prior can be used despite
its inability to represent asymmetric shrinkage.

1. **Foreign marginal VAR.** Estimate $f_t = c_f + \sum_l A_l^{ff} f_{t-l}
   + \varepsilon^f_t$ as a self-contained $n_f$-variable conjugate VAR on
   foreign lags only (§6.1), with its own SOC/DIO dummies. This makes
   $A_l^{fd}=0$ exact.
2. **Domestic conditional regression.** Regress
   $$
   d_t = c_d + \sum_{l=1}^p B_l^{d}\, y_{t-l} + G^\top f_t + \varepsilon^d_t
   $$
   on the intercept, **all** lags, and the **contemporaneous** foreign
   vector $f_t$. The conjugate prior here uses lag variances
   $(\lambda/(\sigma_j l))^2$ and loose contemporaneous-foreign variances
   $\sigma_j^{-2}$; the DIO dummy row is extended with contemporaneous-foreign
   entries $\bar f/\delta_{\text{dio}}$.

For forecasting and conditioning, the two blocks are recombined into the
joint reduced form (`.conj_br_joint()`): with $G$ the
contemporaneous-foreign coefficient rows,

$$
u^f = \varepsilon^f,\quad u^d = G^\top\varepsilon^f + \varepsilon^d,
\qquad
\Sigma_{\text{joint}} =
\begin{pmatrix}
\Sigma_f & \Sigma_f G\\
G^\top\Sigma_f & G^\top\Sigma_f G + \Sigma_d
\end{pmatrix},
$$

and the joint $B$ routes the foreign equations through $G$ into the domestic
rows. This reconstruction is unit-tested against the two-stage simulation.

## Independent Normal–inverse-Wishart Gibbs engine `gibbs` {#sec-gibbs}

The workhorse (§ D3 requires it for block exogeneity). Prior:
$\operatorname{vec}(B)\sim\mathcal N(b_0, V_0)$ with the **diagonal** $V_0$
from the independent Minnesota moments (@eq-minn, so $V_0 =
\operatorname{diag}(\operatorname{vec}(s_0)^2)$ and the block-exog spike
@eq-blockexog-prior), and $\Sigma\sim\mathcal{IW}(S_0,\nu_0)$,
$\nu_0=M+2$. The two full conditionals are Gibbs-sampled
(`fit_gibbs`, `draw_beta_given_sigma`):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i)}\quad
\operatorname{vec}(B)\mid\Sigma,Y &\sim \mathcal N\big(\bar b,\ P^{-1}\big),\\
P &= \Sigma^{-1}\otimes X^\top X + V_0^{-1},\qquad
\bar b = P^{-1}\big(\operatorname{vec}(X^\top Y\,\Sigma^{-1}) + V_0^{-1} b_0\big),\\[4pt]
\text{(ii)}\quad
\Sigma\mid B,Y &\sim \mathcal{IW}\big(S_0 + E^\top E,\ \nu_0 + T\big),\qquad
E = Y - XB.
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-gibbs}

Step (i) draws $\operatorname{vec}(B) = \bar b + \operatorname{chol}(P)^{-1}z$,
$z\sim\mathcal N(0,I)$. The sampler runs `mcmc.nburn` $=300$ burn-in and
`mcmc.ndraw` $=1500$ retained iterations. The **unrestricted control**
`small_unres` (**D19**) is this engine with the block-exog spike removed
(the prior treats every variable as domestic); it is exempt from the
block-exog gate by construction.

## Steady-state (Villani) engine `ss` {#sec-ss}

The steady-state reparametrisation (Villani 2009) writes the VAR around its
unconditional mean $\psi = \mathbb E[y_t]$,

$$
y_t - \psi = \sum_{l=1}^p A_l\,(y_{t-l}-\psi) + \varepsilon_t,
$$ {#eq-ss}

so that informative priors sit **directly on the long-run levels**:
$\psi\sim\mathcal N(\psi_0,\operatorname{diag}(\text{sd}^2))$ with anchors
(`variables[].ss_mean/ss_sd`, **D5**) — trimmed-mean inflation $2.5\%$ p.a.
(target midpoint), GDP growth $2.8\%$, NAIRU $4.5\%$, neutral cash rate
$3.5\%$, US potential growth, and loose sample-mean anchors for the TWI/ToT.
The lag block $A=(A_1,\dots,A_p)$ carries the independent Minnesota prior
@eq-minn (no intercept), including the block-exog spike.

A three-block Gibbs sampler (`fit_ss`) cycles:

1. **$A\mid\psi,\Sigma$** — on the demeaned data $z_t = y_t-\psi$, the
   linear step @eq-gibbs(i) with no intercept. Explosive draws are rejected
   ($\rho(\mathbf A)\ge 1$) up to five times, else kept and flagged via the
   stability share.
2. **$\psi\mid A,\Sigma$** — write $U = I_M - \sum_{l=1}^p A_l$ and
   $w_t = y_t - \sum_l A_l y_{t-l}$, so that $w_t = U\psi + \varepsilon_t$
   (with error scale $s_t^2\Sigma$ under the COVID weighting $\omega_t=1/s_t$
   of §7). The Gaussian–Gaussian conjugate update gives
   $\psi\mid\cdot \sim \mathcal N(\bar\psi, P_\psi^{-1})$ with
   $$
   P_\psi = \operatorname{diag}\!\big(\text{sd}^{-2}\big)
   + \Big(\textstyle\sum_t \omega_t^2\Big)\,U^\top\Sigma^{-1}U,
   \qquad
   \bar\psi = P_\psi^{-1}\Big(\operatorname{diag}(\text{sd}^{-2})\psi_0
   + U^\top\Sigma^{-1}\textstyle\sum_t \omega_t^2 w_t\Big).
   $$ {#eq-psi}
3. **$\Sigma\mid A,\psi$** — inverse-Wishart on the (weighted) demeaned
   residuals as in @eq-gibbs(ii).

**Scope of block exogeneity (D3).** @eq-psi uses the *full*
$M\times M$ precision $\Sigma^{-1}$, so the joint draw of $\psi$ couples the
foreign and domestic means through the contemporaneous error correlations.
This is the correct Villani full conditional — a foreign-only update would be
wrong — so block exogeneity here constrains the **lag dynamics**
($A^{fd}=0$), not the steady-state means. The domestic forecast therefore
inherits foreign steady states informed by the joint residual covariance, by
design.

## Stochastic-volatility engine `sv` {#sec-sv}

Time-varying volatility (Clark 2011; Carriero–Clark–Marcellino 2019) is
estimated equation-by-equation on a **triangular** factorisation of the
reduced form, ordered foreign-first. Equation $i$ is

$$
y_{i,t} = x_{i,t}^\top \beta_i + \exp\!\big(h_{i,t}/2\big)\,\eta_{i,t},
\qquad
h_{i,t} = \mu_i + \phi_i\,(h_{i,t-1}-\mu_i) + \sigma_{\eta,i}\, u_{i,t},
\quad u_{i,t}\sim\mathcal N(0,1),
$$ {#eq-sv}

where $x_{i,t}$ stacks the intercept, the **allowed** lags, and the
**contemporaneous** values $y_{j,t}$ for $j<i$ (`sv_equation_design()`). For
a foreign equation the allowed regressors are foreign lags and foreign
contemporaneous variables only — dropping the domestic columns entirely, so
block exogeneity is **exact**. Because the per-equation priors are
independent, the joint posterior factorises across equations *exactly*
(not an approximation), which is what keeps the recursive loop feasible.

The conditional innovation $\eta_{i,t}$ is Student-$t$ with
$\nu_i$ degrees of freedom for COVID robustness (**D17**), represented as a
scale mixture $\eta_{i,t} = \sqrt{\tau_{i,t}}\,z_{i,t}$,
$z\sim\mathcal N(0,1)$, with `stochvol`'s auxiliary weights $\tau$
(mean $1$, so $\exp(h_{i,t})$ remains the conditional variance). Priors
(`stochvol::specify_priors`):
$\mu\sim\mathcal N(0,10)$, $\phi\sim 2\,\text{Beta}(20,1.5)-1$,
$\sigma_\eta^2\sim\text{Gamma}(0.5,0.5)$, $\nu\sim\text{Exp}(0.1)$ shifted
past $2$. The coefficient prior is Minnesota-shaped: intercept sd
$10\sigma_i$, lag sd $\lambda(\mathbb 1_{\text{own}}+0.5\,\mathbb
1_{\text{cross}})(\sigma_i/\sigma_j)/l$, contemporaneous-loading sd
$\sigma_i/\sigma_j$.

Each Gibbs sweep (`fit_sv`):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta_i\mid h_i,\tau_i &: \text{ weighted ridge regression with weights }
w_{i,t}=\exp(-h_{i,t}/2)/\sqrt{\tau_{i,t}} \text{ and prior precision } s_0^{-2},\\
(h_i,\mu_i,\phi_i,\sigma_{\eta,i},\nu_i,\tau_i)\mid \text{resid}_i &:
\text{ one }\texttt{stochvol::svsample\_fast\_cpp}\text{ update on }
\text{resid}_i = y_i - X_i\beta_i.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Adaptive thinning (D22).** At short samples an equation's own-lag chain
can mix slowly ($\beta$ and the volatility path are strongly coupled). If the
own-lag effective sample size falls below the convergence gate ($50$), that
equation is re-run with $3\times$ then $9\times$ thinning until it clears —
the same philosophy as the UCSV Monte-Carlo-precision gate (§8); the final
ESS is reported honestly.

# COVID-period treatment {#sec-covid}

Three quarters of 2020 are $5$–$30\sigma$ events that, untreated, wreck
constant-volatility coefficient estimates, contaminate the Minnesota scale
calibration, and (in persistent-SV models) get misread as a lasting
volatility regime (Lenza–Primiceri 2022; Hartwig 2024; CCMM 2024). The suite
applies three coordinated mechanisms (`config.covid`, **D17**).

## Lenza–Primiceri volatility scaling (constant-volatility engines)

Augment @eq-var with a deterministic volatility path $s_t$,

$$
y_t = c + \sum_{l=1}^p A_l y_{t-l} + s_t\,\varepsilon_t,\qquad
s_t = \begin{cases}
\bar s_q & t\in\text{COVID quarter } q\ (2020\text{Q1–Q3})\\[2pt]
1 + (\bar s_{\text{last}}-1)\,\rho^{\,j} & t = T_0+j\ \text{after the last COVID quarter}\\[2pt]
1 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$ {#eq-lp}

(`covid_s_path()`). Dividing each observation equation by $s_t$ restores a
homoskedastic VAR — equivalently, GLS row weights $\omega_t = 1/s_t$ on
$(Y,X)$ — so **every** constant-volatility engine, including the conjugate
Kronecker one, applies unchanged. The per-quarter scales
$\bar s_q\in\{1,2,4,8,16,32,64\}$ (`covid.scale_grid`) and the decay
$\rho\in\{0,0.4,0.8\}$ (`covid.decay_grid`) are estimated by maximising the
conjugate log marginal likelihood of the rescaled data *including the
Jacobian* of the rescaling (`.lp_logml`),

$$
\log p_{\text{LP}}(Y\mid \bar s,\rho) =
\log p\big(Y^{\omega}\mid \text{conjugate model}\big) \;-\; M\sum_t \log s_t,
$$ {#eq-lp-ml}

where $Y^\omega, X^\omega$ are the weighted data, by coordinate descent on
the grids. This is done **at every forecast origin** on data up to the origin
(`estimate_covid_scales()`), so it is fully recursive and look-ahead-free.
The Minnesota $\sigma_i$ and the GLP $\lambda$ (§5) use the same weighted
data, closing Hartwig's contamination channel. On real Australian data the
recursive estimates stabilise at $(\bar s)=(1,8,4)$ for 2020Q1–Q3.

At COVID-era origins the predictive shocks carry the decaying future scale
path $s^{\text{fut}}_{1:H}$ (`covid_s_future()`), so the density bands widen
appropriately rather than snapping back to pre-COVID width — the LP
density-forecast payoff.

## SV-$t$ errors (SV engine)

The SV engine does **not** use the row weights; instead the Student-$t$
innovations of @eq-sv absorb the outliers through the transitory scale
mixture $\tau$ rather than the persistent log-volatility process — the CCMM
SV-$t$ specification, which performs within $0$–$2\%$ of their preferred
SVO-$t$ (`covid.sv_t_errors: true`).

## `dummy` alternative and robustness

Setting `covid.treatment: dummy` sends $s_t\to 10^3$ at the COVID quarters,
equivalent to dropping those rows from the likelihood (Schorfheide–Song
2024), with $s^{\text{fut}}=1$; its documented cost is predictive bands stuck
at pre-COVID widths. COVID observations always remain in the forecast
jump-off (only the likelihood contribution is treated). A robustness script
(`scripts/covid_robustness_2021q3.R`) re-runs the full OOS with 2021Q3 added
to the treated set; the effect is within noise (CRPS ratios $0.99$–$1.01$),
so the 2020Q1–Q3 window stands (**D22**).

# Forecasting: iterated density simulation {#sec-forecast}

All members produce **iterated** multi-step density forecasts
(Marcellino–Stock–Watson 2006, **D7**): the one-step model is rolled forward,
integrating over both parameter uncertainty (the posterior draws) and shock
uncertainty (fresh innovations each step). For each retained draw
$(B^{(d)},\Sigma^{(d)})$ and each horizon $s=1,\dots,H$ ($H=12$),
`.iterate_path()` computes

$$
y^{(d)}_{t+s} = c^{(d)} + \sum_{l=1}^p A^{(d)}_l\, y^{(d)}_{t+s-l}
+ s^{\text{fut}}_s\,\operatorname{chol}(\Sigma^{(d)})^\top z_{s},
\qquad z_s\sim\mathcal N(0,I_M),
$$ {#eq-iterate}

with $y_{t+s-l}$ replaced by realized data for $s-l\le 0$. This yields a
predictive sample of `mcmc.forecast_draws` $=1000$ paths per member per
origin; posterior draws are recycled across the full $N$ (not just the first
$1000$) so the density integrates over the whole posterior. For the SV engine
the innovation is $\exp(h^{(d)}_{i,t+s}/2)\,\eta$ with $h$ propagated by
@eq-sv and $\eta$ a **variance-standardised** Student-$t$ draw,
$\eta = t_{\nu}\sqrt{(\nu-2)/\nu}$, so that $\exp(h)$ is exactly the
conditional variance (the standardisation is essential — an unstandardised
$t_\nu$ would inflate every SV predictive variance by $\nu/(\nu-2)$;
independently Monte-Carlo-verified against `stochvol`). For the steady-state
engine the recursion runs in demeaned units $z^{(d)}_{t+s}$ and adds
$\psi^{(d)}$ back.

Predictive quantiles for fan charts are empirical quantiles of the path
array (`fan_quantiles()`); the year-ended and cumulative measures of §2.3 are
formed from the joint paths so their within-horizon dependence is exact.

# Conditional forecasting: Waggoner–Zha and forecast profiles {#sec-cond}

The suite conditions the density forecast on imposed future paths — the
central-bank scenario exercise (**D21/D23**). Different variables may be
pinned over different horizons: the **global block over the entire projection
window**, but domestic nowcast variables (CPI, unemployment) for **one
quarter only**. Each imposed (variable, quarter) pair is one linear
constraint, so ragged profiles are handled by the same machinery.

## The exact Gaussian conditional (Waggoner–Zha 1999)

Stack the future shocks $u=(u_1^\top,\dots,u_H^\top)^\top$ with
$u\sim\mathcal N(0, C)$, $C = \operatorname{blockdiag}(s_1^2\Sigma,\dots,
s_H^2\Sigma)$. The moving-average representation of @eq-var gives the future
path as a linear function of $u$ with weights $\Psi_k$ satisfying

$$
\Psi_0 = I_M,\qquad
\Psi_k = \sum_{l=1}^{\min(k,p)} A_l\,\Psi_{k-l}
\quad(\texttt{.ma\_weights}),
$$

so that $y_{t+s} = m_{t+s} + \sum_{j=1}^{s}\Psi_{s-j}\,u_j$, where $m$ is the
deterministic (zero-shock) path (`.deterministic_path()`). A restriction that
variable $c_j$ equals a target value at horizon $s$ is the linear constraint

$$
\underbrace{\big[\Psi_{s-j}\big]_{c_j,\cdot}}_{\text{row of }R}\ \text{summed over } j\le s
\;=\; r,
\qquad r = \text{target} - m_{s,c_j},
$$

i.e. $R\,u = r$ with one row per imposed cell (`.cond_matrix()` builds the
$H\times M$ constraint grid; NA entries are free). The **exact conditional
draw** of the shocks given $Ru=r$ is the Gaussian conditioning formula

$$
u^\ast = u + C R^\top \big(R C R^\top\big)^{-1}\,(r - R u),
\qquad u\sim\mathcal N(0,C),
$$ {#eq-wz}

after which the path is iterated forward with $u^\ast$
(`.wz_conditional_path()`). Because @eq-wz is applied **per posterior draw**,
parameter uncertainty is retained; only the shocks are constrained.
Non-conditioned variables respond to the imposed path through both the lag
dynamics ($\Psi$) and the contemporaneous error covariance ($C$), and the
un-pinned quarters of a partially-pinned variable revert to model-driven
forecasts *consistent with* the pin. Unit tests confirm the pinned path binds
exactly and the conditional mean/variance match the closed-form Gaussian
update.

The Gaussian engines (`gibbs`, `ss`, `conj_br`) use @eq-wz exactly
(`conj_br` via the joint reduced form of §4.2). The SV engine has
non-Gaussian, time-varying shock variances, so no exact Gaussian conditional
exists; it falls back to hard substitution (overwrite the conditioned
variable each step, logged) — the D15 approximation.

## Forecast profiles

Profiles are supplied as data (`report.conditional.profiles_file`, a CSV of
`variable, date|h, value`; `read_forecast_profiles()`), inline, or in the
legacy single-variable form. Rows outside the forecast window are dropped
with a warning so a file survives the panel end moving; profiles for
variables a member does not model are dropped for that member. The scenario
pools the VAR members with equal weights and writes
`forecast_table_conditional.csv` with an `imposed` flag per cell. The bundled
`data/example_forecast_profiles.csv` pins the full 2026Q2–2028Q2 path of the
foreign block and a one-quarter nowcast for CPI and unemployment.

# Forecast evaluation {#sec-eval}

## Pseudo-real-time recursive design

The evaluation re-lives history (`run_oos_member`, **D11**). Origins are the
last `evaluation.max_origins` $=60$ quarters ending at $T-1$, starting at
$\lceil 0.45\,T\rceil$ (`first_origin_frac`), i.e. ~2011 onward. At each
origin $t$ **every member is re-estimated on $y_{1:t}$ only** — including the
GLP $\lambda$ selection and the COVID scale estimation — and forecasts
$h=1,\dots,12$. This is expanding-window by default (`evaluation.window`;
rolling optional). Final-vintage data is used (real-time vintages are not
reliably key-free; the caveat is stated loudly, **D11**).

**No look-ahead is a correctness property, not a convention.** The single
slice point is `harness_forecast()`; an automated test corrupts all
post-origin data by $\times 10^6$ and requires bit-identical forecast draws
(`test_no_lookahead`, one member per engine, including an origin whose horizon
spans a COVID quarter). Combination weights at $t$ use only scores whose
realization was observable by $t$ ($s+h\le t$, §10).

## Point and density scores

For a predictive sample $\{x^{(d)}\}_{d=1}^{N}$ and realization $y$
(`safe_crps`, `safe_logs` via `scoringRules`):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{RMSE:}\quad & \sqrt{\tfrac1n\textstyle\sum (\hat y - y)^2},\quad
\hat y = \tfrac1N\textstyle\sum_d x^{(d)}\ \text{(predictive mean)},\\[4pt]
\textbf{CRPS:}\quad & \operatorname{CRPS}(F,y)=\mathbb E_F|X-y|-\tfrac12\mathbb E_F|X-X'|
\ \approx\ \tfrac1N\textstyle\sum_d|x^{(d)}-y| - \tfrac{1}{2N^2}\textstyle\sum_{d,e}|x^{(d)}-x^{(e)}|,\\[4pt]
\textbf{log score:}\quad & \log \hat p(y),\quad \hat p\ \text{a Gaussian-kernel density of the sample}.
\end{aligned}
$$

CRPS is reported alongside the log score because the two diverge on
outlier-heavy windows: the log score is dominated by a few tail realizations
(e.g. 2020), CRPS much less so. Both are computed for the **q**, **ye** and
**cum** measures of §2.3, by horizon; the level view (**cum** for growth
targets, **q** for rates) is the headline (`summarise_scores`,
`.level_view`). A COVID-excluded score table is also written (the exclusion
window $=$ 2020Q1–2021Q2, `covid_exclusion_dates()`).

## Calibration: PIT and moment tests

The probability integral transform is $\text{PIT} = \hat F(y) =
\tfrac1N\sum_d \mathbb 1\{x^{(d)}\le y\}$; a calibrated forecaster has uniform
PITs. At $h=1$ a $\chi^2$ test on quintile bins is used (`check_calibration`).
For $h>1$ the overlapping forecast windows make PITs serially correlated, so
the $\chi^2$ test is invalid; instead the first two PIT moments are tested
(Knüppel 2015, **D22**, `pit_moment_tests`) with a Newey–West($h-1$)
variance:

$$
\text{location: } \mathbb E[\text{PIT}]=\tfrac12
\quad(\text{bias}),
\qquad
\text{dispersion: } \mathbb E\big[(\text{PIT}-\tfrac12)^2\big]=\tfrac1{12}
\quad(\text{too wide / too narrow}).
$$

## Diebold–Mariano tests

For two members' losses $L^1_t, L^2_t$ (squared error or CRPS) on aligned
origins, the loss differential $d_t = L^1_t - L^2_t$ is tested for zero mean
(`dm_test`) with the Harvey small-sample correction and a Newey–West variance
truncated at the forecast-overlap lag $L$:

$$
\text{DM} = k\cdot\frac{\bar d}{\sqrt{\hat V/n}},\qquad
\hat V = \gamma_0 + 2\sum_{l=1}^{L}\Big(1-\frac{l}{L+1}\Big)\gamma_l,\qquad
k = \sqrt{\frac{n+1-2h+h(h-1)/n}{n}},
$$

with $\gamma_l$ the sample autocovariances of $d_t$, $L=h-1$ for the
quarterly/level measures and $L=h+3$ for the overlapping year-ended sums, and
$p$-values from $t_{n-1}$. Members are compared against the AR(4) and
random-walk benchmarks (`dm_vs_reference`). These losses are high-variance, so
the tests have low power at long horizons — absence of significance is weak
evidence.

# Density combination {#sec-combine}

The production forecast is a **linear pool** of the member densities. For
weights $w=(w_1,\dots,w_M)$ on the simplex, the pooled predictive density,
log score, PIT and CRPS are

$$
\hat p_{\text{pool}}(y) = \sum_m w_m\,\hat p_m(y),
\qquad
\log \hat p_{\text{pool}}(y) = \operatorname{logsumexp}_m\big(\log w_m + \log\hat p_m(y)\big),
$$

$$
\text{PIT}_{\text{pool}} = \sum_m w_m\,\text{PIT}_m
\ \ (\text{exact mixture CDF}),
\qquad
\text{CRPS}_{\text{pool}} = \operatorname{CRPS}\big(\text{mixture sample},\,y\big),
$$

where the mixture sample is drawn by allocating $N_{\text{mix}}$ draws across
members via a multinomial with probabilities $w$ (`combine_all`), and
$\operatorname{logsumexp}$ is the stable log-sum-exp (`logsumexp()`).

## Weight schemes {#sec-schemes}

Weights are estimated **per target variable and per horizon bucket**
(near $1$–$4$, medium $5$–$8$, far $9$–$12$; `horizon_buckets`) and only from
training scores realized by the origin ($s+h\le t$; `.train_scores`). Let
$\ell_m$ and $\bar C_m$ be the discounted training log-score sum and
discounted mean CRPS with forgetting $\gamma=0.95$ (`forgetting`), age
$= t-(s+h)$:

$$
\ell_m = \sum_{s} \gamma^{\,t-(s+h)}\log\hat p_m,\qquad
\bar C_m = \frac{\sum_s \gamma^{\,t-(s+h)}\,\text{CRPS}_{m,s}}{\sum_s \gamma^{\,t-(s+h)}}.
$$

The five schemes (`combination.schemes`):

| Scheme | Weight rule (before shrinkage) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `equal` | $w_m = 1/M$ | the combination-puzzle benchmark; recommended default |
| `logscore` | $w_m \propto \exp(\ell_m)$ (softmax) | Jore–Mitchell–Vahey; tail-sensitive |
| `crps` | $w_m \propto 1/\bar C_m$ | outlier-robust performance weighting (**D20**) |
| `pool` | $\arg\max_w \sum_s \gamma^{\text{age}}\log\!\sum_m w_m \hat p_{m,s}$ | optimal prediction pool (Hall–Mitchell / Geweke–Amisano) |
| `bma` | $w_m \propto \exp\!\big(\sum_s \log\hat p_{m,s}\big)$ at the bucket's near-edge horizon | **diagnostic only**; concentrates on one model |

The **optimal pool** (`optimal_pool_weights`) maximises the discounted
historical *pooled* log score over the simplex, softmax-parametrised
($w = \operatorname{softmax}(0,\theta)$) and solved by BFGS — unlike BMA it
does not degenerate to a single model ("all models are false but useful").

All schemes except BMA are **shrunk toward equal weights** (`.shrink`, the
combination-puzzle guard):

$$
w \leftarrow \frac{\kappa}{M} + (1-\kappa)\,w,\qquad \kappa = 0.3\ (\texttt{shrink\_kappa}),
$$

and equal weights are used until `min_train_origins` $=8$ training origins
exist.

## Training loss and the COVID window (D20)

Two robustness choices, both traced to the 2026-07 audit finding that
untreated log-score weights handed the far-horizon GDP bucket to the random
walk on the strength of a few 2020 quarters:

- `train_measure: level` — performance weights train on the **same loss the
  headline reports**: the cumulative-level (`cum`) scores for growth-modelled
  targets (`q` at $h=1$, where they coincide) and the rate level for
  level-modelled targets. Training on the quarterly measure while reporting
  the level measure is a mismatch that let a member good at one but poor at
  the other earn weight.
- `exclude_covid_train: true` — score observations *realized* in the COVID
  exclusion window (2020Q1–2021Q2) are dropped from weight training. This is
  the weight-estimation analogue of the D17 principle: treat the outlier's
  likelihood contribution, keep everything else (the pre-specified-dates
  principle).

# Event probabilities {#sec-events}

Policy questions are answered directly from the pooled predictive
distribution (`event_probabilities`, **D22**). For a linear pool the event
probability is exactly the weighted average of member event probabilities,
and member probabilities are computed from each member's **joint path draws**
(so the year-ended sums preserve within-path dependence):

$$
\Pr(\text{event}) = \sum_m w_m\,\Pr\nolimits_m(\text{event}),
\qquad
\Pr\nolimits_m(\text{event}) = \tfrac1N\textstyle\sum_d \mathbb 1\{\text{event holds on path } d\}.
$$

The configured events (`report.events`) are P(year-ended GDP growth $<0$),
P(year-ended trimmed-mean inflation in $[2,3]\%$), and P(unemployment rises
$\ge 0.75$pp from the jump-off), reported by horizon.

# Benchmarks {#sec-bench}

Four univariate benchmarks are both pool members and the bar every VAR must
clear (`fit_benchmark`, **D8/D9**):

- **`rw`** — random walk on the transformed series, Gaussian increments with
  the (COVID-weighted) historical change sd.
- **`ar4`** — Bayesian AR(4) per variable, conjugate normal–inverse-gamma
  with Minnesota lag shrinkage (sd $0.5/l$) and the own-lag prior mean
  $\delta_v$ (**D22**); iterated density forecasts with a stationarity
  truncation ($\min|\text{root}|>1$) on the coefficient draws.
- **`ucsv`** — Stock–Watson unobserved-components stochastic volatility per
  variable: local-level trend $\tau_t$ plus transitory noise, both with
  time-varying variances, estimated by FFBS + `stochvol`. Three deliberate
  deviations force robustness at COVID-sized endpoint outliers (**D9**):
  $t$-distributed transitory errors, a **constant** trend-shock variance with
  a conjugate inverse-gamma update (removing the trend-vol pile-up), and a
  tight vol-of-vol prior. Convergence is gated on the Monte-Carlo standard
  error of the trend endpoint relative to the one-step predictive sd, with an
  adaptive triple-thinning retry — the forecast-relevant quantity, not raw
  parameter ESS.
- **`ucmean`** — Gaussian density centred on the expanding-sample mean with
  the sample variance (COVID-weighted).

# Diagnostics and correctness gates {#sec-diagnostics}

The pipeline refuses to emit results unless every §9-style property passes
(`assert_diagnostics`, a hard gate):

| Property | Check | Threshold |
|---|---|---|
| Block exogeneity | $\max_l\|A_l^{fd}\|_{\max}$ per fit | $<10^{-2}$ (exempt: `small_unres`) |
| MCMC convergence | own-lag effective sample size | $>50$ (or NA when `coda` absent) |
| Stability | companion eigenvalue share $\rho(\mathbf A)<1.05$ | reported |
| Forecast sanity | finite, non-explosive tails, median in a data-scaled band | pass |
| No look-ahead | corrupt post-origin data $\Rightarrow$ identical draws | bit-identical |
| Reproducibility | same master seed $\Rightarrow$ identical draws | bit-identical |
| Calibration | PIT uniform / moment tests | reported |

Reproducibility rests on a single master seed (`master_seed`) from which
every stochastic task (member $\times$ origin, final forecasts, mixture
resampling) derives its own seed by hashing (`derive_seed`), so results are
independent of the parallel scheduling (**D13**). The recursive OOS is
disk-cached keyed by a hash of the estimation-relevant config *and* the
estimation R sources (`config_hash`), so combination experiments cost seconds
(**D14**).

# Reference: symbols and config keys {#sec-symbols}

| Symbol | Meaning | Default / config |
|---|---|---|
| $M,\ p,\ K$ | variables, lags, regressors $=1+Mp$ | `suite[].lags` |
| $n_f,\ n_d$ | foreign / domestic block sizes | `variables[].block` |
| $\lambda$ | Minnesota overall tightness | `prior.lambda` (`auto`/0.05/0.4) |
| $\kappa$ | cross-variable shrinkage | $0.5$ (`cross`) |
| $\alpha$ | lag-decay exponent | $1$ (`lag_decay`) |
| $\theta_c$ | intercept looseness | $10$ (`intercept_scale`) |
| $\underline s$ | block-exog prior sd | $10^{-4}$ (`mcmc.block_exog_prior_sd`) |
| $\delta_v$ | own-lag prior mean | `variables[].delta` |
| $\sigma_i$ | AR-residual scale | `ar_sigmas()` |
| $\nu_0,\ S_0$ | IW prior dof / scale | $M+2$, $\operatorname{diag}(\sigma^2)(\nu_0-M-1)$ |
| $\psi_0,\ \text{sd}$ | steady-state anchor | `variables[].ss_mean/ss_sd` |
| $\bar s_q,\ \rho$ | COVID scales / decay | `covid.scale_grid`, `covid.decay_grid` |
| $\gamma$ | forgetting factor | $0.95$ (`forgetting`) |
| $\kappa$ (combo) | shrink toward equal | $0.3$ (`shrink_kappa`) |
| $N$ | posterior / predictive draws | `mcmc.ndraw`=1500, `forecast_draws`=1000 |
| $H$ | max horizon | $12$ (`horizons`) |

# References {#sec-refs}

Bańbura, Giannone & Reichlin (2010), *Large Bayesian VARs*, J. Applied
Econometrics. — Bloor & Matheson (2008, 2010), block-recursive SOE VARs,
RBNZ. — Carriero, Clark & Marcellino (2019), *Large VARs with SV*, J.
Econometrics. — CCMM (2024), outlier-robust SV, REStat. — Clark (2011), SV
and density forecasts. — Cushman & Zha (1997), *Identifying monetary policy
in a small open economy*, J. Monetary Economics. — Doan, Litterman & Sims
(1984). — Geweke & Amisano (2011), *Optimal prediction pools*, J.
Econometrics. — Giannone, Lenza & Primiceri (2015), *Prior selection for
vector autoregressions*, REStat. — Gneiting & Raftery (2007), *Strictly
proper scoring rules*, JASA. — Hall & Mitchell (2007), *Combining density
forecasts*, IJF. — Hartwig (2024), COVID and Minnesota scale calibration,
SNDE. — Harvey, Leybourne & Newbold (1997), DM small-sample correction. —
Jore, Mitchell & Vahey (2010), *Combining forecast densities*, J. Applied
Econometrics. — Knüppel (2015), *Evaluating the calibration of multi-step
density forecasts*, J. Business & Economic Statistics. — Lenza & Primiceri
(2022), *How to estimate a VAR after March 2020*, J. Applied Econometrics. —
Litterman (1986), *Forecasting with Bayesian VARs*, JBES. — Marcellino,
Stock & Watson (2006), *Iterated vs direct multi-step forecasts*, J.
Econometrics. — Schorfheide & Song (2024), pandemic-period VARs. — Villani
(2009), *Steady-state priors for VARs*, J. Applied Econometrics. — Waggoner &
Zha (1999), *Conditional forecasts in dynamic multivariate models*, REStat. —
Zha (1999), *Block recursion and structural VARs*, J. Econometrics.
